sources: https://arxiv.org/html/2503.11690v1 - The Impact of Meteorological Factors on Crop Price Volatility in India
https://epubs.icar.org.in - Understanding price volatility and seasonality in agricultural commodities in India
general_factors: Vegetable prices rise during off-season due to supply shortages.
Market arrivals (quantity brought to mandi) strongly influence modal price - higher arrivals typically lower prices.
Festival months (Diwali, Holi, Pongal, Onam) often increase demand and prices for specific commodities.
Rainfall and monsoon patterns significantly affect mandi arrivals and pricing.
Transportation costs and fuel prices impact final commodity prices.
Cold storage availability affects price stability for perishables.
Government minimum support prices (MSP) set price floors for certain crops.
Export-import policies and trade restrictions affect domestic prices.
Pest infestations and crop diseases can cause sudden supply shortages.
Irrigation availability determines crop yield and subsequent market supply.
Climate-induced disruptions can have profound consequences for food security and income stability.
Temperature anomalies and precipitation patterns are pivotal in understanding food price volatility.
Export-oriented crops like soybean are more sensitive to global disruptions than domestic vegetables.
Extreme precipitation makes economic inequality worse in nations that rely heavily on agriculture.
Weather conditions, especially extreme weather events, significantly impact agricultural productivity and pricing.

summer: Mango, watermelon, and cucumber prices drop due to peak production.
Leafy vegetables become scarce and expensive due to heat.
Onion prices typically rise as stored stock depletes before new harvest.
monsoon: Vegetable prices often spike due to transportation difficulties.
Leafy greens become expensive due to damage from heavy rains.
Rice transplanting season affects labor availability and costs.
winter: Tomato, cauliflower, cabbage prices drop due to abundant supply.
Potato harvest leads to lower prices in major producing states.
Peas and carrots are at their cheapest during peak season.
post_monsoon: Kharif crop arrivals increase, potentially lowering grain prices.
Onion new crop arrives, stabilizing prices after monsoon spike.
Festival demand increases prices for specific commodities.

onion: Onion prices are highly volatile due to limited storage life.
Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Madhya Pradesh are major onion producing states.
Rabi onion (harvested March-May) has better storage life than Kharif.
Export bans are often imposed when domestic prices spike.
tomato: Tomato is highly perishable with price swings of 200-300% within weeks.
Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, and Maharashtra are major producers.
Prices peak during monsoon due to crop damage and transport issues.
Cold chain infrastructure significantly impacts price stability.
potato: Uttar Pradesh, West Bengal, and Bihar are major potato producers.
Cold storage capacity affects year-round price stability.
Prices typically lowest during February-April harvest season.
Processing industry demand (chips, fries) affects premium variety prices.
rice: Minimum Support Price (MSP) provides price floor for paddy.
Government procurement significantly affects market prices.
Monsoon quality directly impacts Kharif rice production.
Export policies influence domestic price levels.

andhra_pradesh: Major producer of rice, chillies, and vegetables.
Coastal areas affected by cyclones impacting crop production.
Godavari and Krishna delta regions are highly productive.
Strong mandi network with electronic trading adoption.
maharashtra: Leading producer of onions, grapes, and sugarcane.
Nashik is the onion trading hub of India.
Drought-prone regions face frequent supply disruptions.
Well-developed cold chain for export-oriented produce.
karnataka: Major producer of tomatoes, onions, and pulses.
Bangalore market influences South India vegetable prices.
Coffee and spice production in Western Ghats region.
Irrigation from Cauvery and Krishna rivers crucial for production.

market_dynamics: Modal price represents the most frequent transaction price in a mandi.
Price spread between min and max indicates market volatility.
Arrival quantity inversely correlates with price in most cases.
Trader speculation can cause short-term price distortions.
APMC mandi prices serve as benchmark for regional transactions.
eNAM (electronic National Agriculture Market) is improving price discovery.
Wholesale prices typically 30-50% lower than retail prices.
APMC Licensing requires farmers to sell through authorized intermediaries in specified mandis.
In mandi auctions, items are examined, graded, and put up for auction to the highest bidder.
Supply and demand have a major influence on mandi prices, with government MSP affecting the price floor.
The Farmers' Produce Trade and Commerce Act 2020 permits farmers to sell to any buyer and eliminates middlemen.
Activities at Mandi usually begin early in the morning.
Produce such as wheat, potatoes, and onions are kept in mandis or special storage spaces to sell later at better prices.
major_mandis: Azadpur Mandi, Delhi: One of the largest fruit and vegetable markets in Asia, receiving 4000-5000 trucks daily.
Lasalgaon, Maharashtra: Known as the largest onion market in Asia.
Vashi APMC Market, Mumbai: Major market for fruits, vegetables, grains, and pulses.
Unjha Mandi, Gujarat: Known for trading spices, especially cumin and fennel.
Chili Market, Guntur, Andhra Pradesh: One of the largest chili markets in India.
meteorological_impact: Kharif crops like soybean are strictly sown during monsoon onset (June/July) and harvested in October.
Meteorological variables impact soybean price volatility at lags of 3-4 months, consistent with sowing and harvesting periods.
Vegetables like brinjal are cultivated year-round without strict seasonal cycles, showing lower weather correlation.
Weather forecasts can help farmers optimize sowing and harvesting time for better price realization.
Apart from meteorological variables, global factors like COVID-19 also significantly affect price volatility.
Hybrid ARIMAX-LSTM models outperform traditional methods for price forecasting with meteorological data.
